sources.name: Wikipedia — Little Nemo: The Dream Master
sources.notes: Animal possession mechanic confirmed. 7 creatures with different abilities.

sources.name: Speedrun.com — Little Nemo: The Dream Master

notes: Little Nemo is based on Winsor McCay's 'Little Nemo in Slumberland' comic strip.
DEFINING MECHANIC: Nemo feeds candy to animals, then climbs inside them to use their unique abilities.
Each creature gives completely different movement and combat capabilities — the game is essentially 7 games in one body.
Nemo alone is fragile with limited attacks (wand). The animal possession is the primary survival and traversal strategy.
7 possessable animals: Frog, Bee, Gorilla, Lizard, Mole, Mouse, and a few others depending on level.

nemo_base_physics._note: Nemo without an animal host. Fragile and limited.

jump.notes: Standard Capcom NES jump feel. VERIFY.

wand_attack.description: Nemo's pajama wand. Short-range stun — temporarily freezes enemies.
wand_attack.effect: Stun only — does not kill. Stunned enemies can be climbed on.
wand_attack.notes: The wand creates opportunities to possess animals (must feed candy to approach).

candy_throw.description: Nemo can toss candy toward animals. The animal approaches and eats it.
candy_throw.notes: Candy must be collected from the environment. Limited supply.

animal_possession._note: The core game mechanic. Each animal fundamentally changes Nemo's capabilities.
animal_possession.mechanic: Approach an animal and press action. Nemo climbs inside. To exit, press action again — Nemo pops out.

frog.ability: Wall cling. Can stick to vertical surfaces and jump off.
frog.special: Wall cling enables vertical traversal and ceiling access
frog.notes: Wall cling creates access to areas Nemo can't reach alone.

bee.ability: Short-range flight. Press and hold jump to fly temporarily.
bee.special: Can reach heights inaccessible to ground-based movement
bee.notes: Flight duration is limited — not infinite hover. Must land and wait or land to recharge.

gorilla.ability: Throw Nemo at enemies or to reach distant platforms.
gorilla.special: Self-throw mechanic — Nemo can be launched by the gorilla to reach far platforms
gorilla.notes: The gorilla is a combat powerhouse and long-range launcher.

lizard.ability: Wall and ceiling climbing. Slower but accesses surfaces others can't.
lizard.special: Can traverse walls and ceilings — similar to Nemo's wand victim state
lizard.notes: The lizard makes the entire level navigable in ways standard movement doesn't allow.

mole.ability: Dig through soft earth/ground. Access underground passages.
mole.special: Digging through certain floor tiles creates passages
mole.notes: Enables subterranean routing not available to surface characters.

mouse.ability: Small hitbox. Can fit through tight passages.
mouse.special: Access passages other animals can't fit through
mouse.notes: The mouse's small size makes many tight-squeeze sections trivial.

candy_system.description: Candy is the resource for animal possession. Collect candy from environment, use to bait animals.
candy_system.supply: Limited — found in specific locations in each level
notes: Running out of candy means no animal possession until more is found.
Some levels require using the right animal at the right time — candy management is strategic.
Candy can also be thrown at enemies to distract them (VERIFY).
Each world emphasizes different animals. Bee levels have lots of vertical challenges. Mole levels have underground paths.
The level design telegraphs which animal will be useful through the environmental obstacles present.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Little Nemo's animal possession is one of the earliest 'character switching' systems in platformers, predating modern implementations by decades. The key design insight: possession is temporary and costly (requires candy), so it's a resource decision rather than a permanent mode. Players choose WHEN to possess, not just WHICH animal.

Each animal represents a different traversal philosophy: Bee = vertical access. Frog = wall access. Lizard = surface inversion. Mole = underground. Mouse = size-based access. These are five distinct spatial solutions to obstacle design — a complete taxonomy of movement types from 1990.

The wand's stun (not kill) mechanic creates interesting geometry: stunned enemies are temporary platforms. An enemy Nemo can't safely engage becomes furniture if she stuns it. This is 'environmental conversion' — obstacles become tools.

The candy economy ties the possession system to resource scarcity. Even if a perfect animal is available, possession costs candy that might be needed later. This forces players to think ahead about resource consumption — a form of soft-limited inventory management in a simple platformer.

Nemo alone is intentionally weak — her survival depends on possession or avoidance. This framing (the protagonist is fragile; animals are the power) creates a different relationship to combat than 'get stronger weapon.' Instead: 'find the right friend.'
